tone_and_voice: Clinical precision balanced with human dignity. Health survey writing must never reduce respondents to statistics alone -- always connect a number to what it means for a real patient's experience of care. Avoid alarmist language even when findings are negative; health system findings should read as diagnostic, not accusatory.

section_rules.executive_summary: Must state the single most significant health-system finding in the first two sentences. Always distinguish between facility-level findings (specific, actionable) and system-level findings (structural, requiring longer-term response). Never claim a health outcome improved without a specific, respondent-confirmed causal link -- sentiment alone is insufficient evidence of health outcome change.
section_rules.discussion: Must explicitly separate access barriers, service quality, and clinical/medicine-supply findings into distinct analytical threads -- these are different health-system dimensions with different accountable owners and must never be blended into one undifferentiated 'health system performance' narrative.
section_rules.methodology: Must state the channel(s) used for data collection and note any known bias risk (e.g. mobile/SMS-based collection under-represents populations without phone access, a specific limitation for elderly or very remote respondents in health surveys specifically).
section_rules.limitations: Must explicitly address whether self-reported health service experience was validated against any facility-level administrative data. If not, state this as a specific limitation -- self-report alone cannot substitute for clinical outcome data.
section_rules.recommendations: Must be split by response tier appropriate to health systems: facility-level (immediate, no new budget), sub-national health management (medium-term), and national policy (long-term). Never propose a facility-level fix for a finding that is clearly systemic (e.g. medicine supply chain).
section_rules.policy_implications: Only include if findings clearly point to a policy (not operational) gap. A wait-time complaint is operational; a persistent, multi-region medicine stock-out pattern is policy-relevant.
section_rules.lessons_learned: Should reflect on what the OPEN-ENDED response format revealed that a closed-ended satisfaction scale would have missed -- this is a recurring, genuine strength of this platform's methodology and should be named as such.
section_rules.conclusion: Must avoid declaring the health system 'improved' or 'failed' in absolute terms -- health system change is incremental and multi-causal; use precise, bounded language ('this round's evidence indicates...').
section_rules.annexes: Should include the exact question wording used, since health survey question phrasing materially affects comparability across rounds (e.g. 'wait time' vs. 'time from arrival to being seen' are different measures).
section_rules.evidence_style: Every quantitative claim must cite the specific sentiment/topic count it derives from. Verbatim quotes should be used to illustrate, never to substitute for, the quantitative finding.

common_risks: under-representation of populations without phone/mobile access
seasonal variation in facility conditions not captured in a single round
conflating access barriers with quality-of-care barriers
typical_recommendations: outreach/mobile clinic models for distance-affected populations
supply chain monitoring to sustain medicine availability gains
time-and-motion studies before committing to a staffing fix
sector_knowledge.typical_donor_expectations: Donors funding health programmes expect explicit linkage to WHO-referenced service standards where applicable, and want data-quality caveats (self-report vs. clinical validation) stated plainly rather than implied.

forbidden_behaviors: Never state a specific clinical outcome (e.g. mortality, morbidity rates) unless that data was actually collected -- sentiment surveys measure EXPERIENCE, not clinical outcomes.
Never cite a specific WHO guideline document or number without independent verification that it exists and applies.
Never claim a medicine availability or supply chain improvement is permanent based on a single survey round -- always frame as a point-in-time finding requiring continued monitoring.
